_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: In infants with critical valvular AS with duct dependent circulation
Children with isolated valvular AS with LV dysfunction, irrespective of valve gradient
Resting peak gradient of more than 50 mmHg on catheterization or more than 64 mmHg on echo
Symptomatic with peak gradient > 40 mmHg on catheterization

preauth.label: Clinical notes with planned line of treatment

claim.label: Procedure / Operative notes with indication of procedure
Post procedure stills of ECHO with report

ppd_preauth.q: Was the Echo report suggestive of Aortic stenosis?

cpd_claim.q: Does the Post procedure still of ECHO show increase in Aortic valve area?
Is there a Detailed Discharge Summary mentioning date of follow-up submitted?

common_queries: Echo report not suggestive of Aortic stenosis at pre-authorization
Resting peak gradient or symptomatic peak gradient not meeting clinical thresholds as per guidelines
Clinical notes missing or lacking planned line of treatment
Post-procedure ECHO does not show an increase in Aortic valve area
Invoice for balloon used not submitted
Detailed Discharge Summary missing or not mentioning date of follow-up

clinical_key_pointers: Aortic stenosis is a congenital or acquired reduction in the area of the aortic valve, resulting in obstruction of the blood flow from the left ventricle to the aorta. Aortic stenosis accounts for 2-5% of all congenital heart defects and is a potentially life-threatening disorder. Degenerative etiology is present in majority of cases after age of 60 years.

Patients with mild aortic stenosis are usually asymptomatic. Patients become symptomatic when AS becomes severe, defined by aortic valve area less than 1.0 cm2 (0.6 cm2/m2) and echocardiography is procedure of choice for evaluation. The symptoms are chest pain, syncope and dyspnoea.

Common Signs and Symptoms of Aortic Stenosis include: Symptoms (Exertional Dyspnea, Angina, Exertional Syncope, Sudden death) and Signs (Ejectional systolic murmur, Slow-rising carotid pulse, Narrow pulse pressure, Thrusting apex beat, Signs of pulmonary venous congestion (eg. Crepitations)).

Balloon valvuloplasty has evolved as procedure of choice in severe non-calcific isolated valvular aortic stenosis, particularly in children and adolescent. A re-intervention is required in majority of patients in long term.
